description:
You are acting as an automation-specialist who has been asked to demonstrate basic
regular-expression log filtering on a Linux workstation.

Initial setup
-------------
1. A directory called /home/user/logs already exists.
2. Inside that directory there is a single text file named app.log.
   • You do NOT need to create either the directory or this file—the instructor has
     provided them for you.

What you must do
----------------
Using no more than 3–4 shell commands (pipelines count as one), perform all of the
following:

A. Extract from /home/user/logs/app.log every log entry that simultaneously meets
   both of these criteria:
   1. The timestamp starts with the calendar date 2021-09-15
   2. The log-level token is the literal string “ERROR”
   Typical lines look like this (format reference only):
     2021-09-15 11:01:17,890 ERROR [module] - Something broke
   • Use a single regular expression (e.g. grep ‑E) rather than two sequential greps.

B. Save the lines that match into a new file named
   /home/user/logs/error_2021-09-15.log

C. Count how many lines you just wrote (i.e., how many “ERROR” entries occurred
   on 2021-09-15) and write that number to a second file called
   /home/user/logs/analysis.txt in the exact format shown below (no quotation marks):
   Total ERROR entries on 2021-09-15: <number>
   The file must end with a single trailing newline.

D. Display the same line you placed in analysis.txt to STDOUT so the human observer
   can immediately see the result.

Output format requirements that the autograder will check
---------------------------------------------------------
1. /home/user/logs/error_2021-09-15.log
   • Plain-text file
   • Contains only the full log lines that meet the two criteria above, in their
     original order. No extra blank lines.

2. /home/user/logs/analysis.txt
   • One and only one line
   • Matches the exact pattern:
       Total ERROR entries on 2021-09-15: <number>\n

3. The exact same single summary line must appear in the terminal output produced
   by your commands.

Hints (do NOT reveal commands)
------------------------------
• A single extended-regex grep (or awk) can match a date prefix and the literal
  “ERROR” in one pass.
• Remember to redirect rather than append when creating analysis.txt so the file
  contains only the required line.

Success criteria
----------------
The grading script will confirm all of the following:
1. /home/user/logs/error_2021-09-15.log exists and has the correct three matching
   lines and nothing else.
2. /home/user/logs/analysis.txt exists and contains exactly:
     Total ERROR entries on 2021-09-15: 3
   followed by a newline.
3. The terminal output (captured from your last command) includes the exact same
   line.

2021-09-15 11:02:00,123 WARN  [auth]    - Slow response

2021-09-16 09:15:00,001 ERROR [db]      - Connection lost
2021-09-15 12:00:00,000 INFO  [module]  - Ended
2021-09-15 13:15:10,678 ERROR [payment] - Payment failed
2021-09-15 14:20:30,789 DEBUG [payment] - Debug message
2021-09-14 15:10:50,987 ERROR [module]  - Unexpected error

Expected artifacts after successful task
----------------------------------------
1. /home/user/logs/error_2021-09-15.log ; mode 644
   Expected content (exactly 3 lines, each ending in \n):

2021-09-15 11:01:17,890 ERROR [auth]    - Invalid credentials
2021-09-15 11:05:43,456 ERROR [auth]    - User locked out
2021-09-15 13:15:10,678 ERROR [payment] - Payment failed

2. /home/user/logs/analysis.txt ; mode 644
   Expected content (exactly 1 line ending in \n):
Total ERROR entries on 2021-09-15: 3

3. STDOUT must contain the string:
Total ERROR entries on 2021-09-15: 3
-->
